He founded the great monastery of Debre Libanos, which became and remains the spiritual heartland of Ethiopian Christianity, the mother of the monastic tradition in the center of the country, and the school in which countless generations of priests, deacons, and monks have been formed.

His intercessions were not confined to the living: tradition records that he descended into the realm of the dead — following in the footsteps of his Lord — and that by his prayers souls were released from the places of suffering into the light of Paradise. This extraordinary claim is not made carelessly by the Church; it is an expression of the Tewahedo conviction that the communion of saints is not a metaphor but a living reality, that the saints who have passed into the fullness of Christ's life continue to act in this world with a power that is greater, not lesser, than what they possessed in the body. When the faithful of Ethiopia call upon Tekle Haymanot, they call upon one who is alive, active, and near — the Light of Ethiopia burning as brightly in heaven as it ever did on the roads of Shewa.

St. Titus the Apostle and St. Bartholomew the Apostle join Tekle Haymanot in today's exalted assembly, weaving the particular glory of Ethiopia's greatest monastic into the global apostolic witness of the universal Church. Titus was the beloved companion of St. Paul, the faithful co-worker to whom one of the Pastoral Epistles is addressed, the first bishop of Crete, who spent his life translating the apostolic vision into the practical structures of communities across the Greek and Cretan world. His presence alongside Tekle Haymanot honors the continuity of the apostolic mission: what Titus did for Crete in the first century, Tekle Haymanot did for Ethiopia in the thirteenth, and the Spirit that animated both of them is one Spirit. St. Bartholomew, commemorated monthly, was the apostle whose mission tradition assigns in part to India and to Armenia — the great apostle of the edges of the known world — and whose monthly presence on the Ethiopian calendar acknowledges that the Church is always on the move toward those who have not yet heard. Together, on this twenty-fifth and luminous day of Nehase, these three apostles — one Ethiopian, one Pauline, one of the original Twelve — stand as witnesses to the single, universal, inexhaustible mission of Christ in the world.

26.title: 26 Nehase — St. Joshua son of Nun & The Assembly
26.narrative:
Nehase

St. Joshua son of Nun (Monthly Commemoration)

Sts. Adrian and Natalia, Martyrs

St. Ibestion the Martyr

On the twenty-sixth day of Nehase, the Church honors St. Joshua son of Nun in his monthly commemoration — and the Ethiopian calendar's inclusion of Joshua among the monthly saints speaks to the Tewahedo tradition's deep rootedness in the entire sweep of scriptural history, from Adam at the beginning of the month to the prophets and judges and kings who appear throughout. Joshua is the man who completed what Moses began: he led the people across the Jordan and into the Promised Land, distributing the inheritance that God had sworn to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. His name in Hebrew — Yehoshua — is the same name that the New Testament renders as Jesus, and the Fathers of the Church universally read Joshua as a type of Christ: the one who brings the people through the waters of death (the Jordan = baptism) and into the inheritance of eternal life (Canaan = the Kingdom of God).

The resonance between Joshua and the conclusion of Nehase is profound. The month has been a journey — from Adam's fall, through the Transfiguration's light, through fifteen days of fast, through the Assumption's glory, through the feast of Ethiopia's greatest saint — and now, on the twenty-sixth day, the figure of the conqueror-inheritor appears to name what this whole journey has been about. The faithful have been crossing a Jordan of their own: the disciplines of the month, the fast of Filseta, the commemorations of martyrs and confessors and prophets, have been a progressive crossing into a wider and more spacious life in God. Joshua stands at the bank and says: cross over; the inheritance is real; the promises of God do not fail.

Sts. Adrian and Natalia are one of the Church's most beloved married-couple martyrdoms. Adrian was a pagan official in Nicomedia who was so struck by the endurance and joy of the Christian prisoners he was guarding that he declared himself a Christian and was imprisoned with them. His wife Natalia, secretly already a Christian, cared for him and the other prisoners during their confinement, and urged him not to recant. He was executed; she survived him and died peacefully, her witness that of the faithful spouse who accompanied her husband to the very threshold of death and saw him through to the other side. Their story is an icon of married holiness: the covenant of husband and wife becomes, in the crucible of persecution, a covenant of mutual martyrdom.

St. Ibestion the Martyr adds his voice to the universal chorus of those who sealed their faith with blood, and together with Adrian and Natalia stands as testimony that no station in life — military official, devoted wife, anonymous believer — exempts the Christian from the call to total witness. As Nehase approaches its final days, Joshua, Adrian, Natalia, and Ibestion together compose a final image of the promised life: the inheritance is real, the crossing is possible, the cost is known, and those who have paid it are not diminished but glorified.

27.title: 27 Nehase — The Monthly Crucifixion & The Assembly
27.narrative:
Nehase

The Monthly Commemoration of the Holy Crucifixion

St. Pimen the Great (Abba Poemen), Desert Father

St. Phanourios the Great Martyr

On the twenty-seventh day of every month, the Ethiopian Church pauses in its ordinary commemorations to stand once more before the Cross. The monthly Crucifixion remembrance is among the most distinctive and theologically profound features of the Ethiopian liturgical calendar: it refuses to let the Passion recede into the past, insisting that the Cross is not a completed event but a perpetually present reality, the axis of all time, the still point around which every month revolves. To stand before the Cross on the twenty-seventh of Nehase, days after the Transfiguration and the Assumption, is to be reminded of what those glorious feasts cost and what they are built upon. The light of Tabor shines from a face marked by thorns. The glorified body of the Virgin is a body drawn from the womb of a people who waited through darkness. Every mountain of glory has Golgotha somewhere in its foundation.

Abba Poemen — Pimen the Great — was one of the most celebrated of all the desert fathers, whose sayings fill a substantial portion of the Apophthegmata Patrum and whose wisdom has shaped the contemplative tradition in every subsequent era. His name means 'shepherd,' and he was indeed a shepherd of souls in the fullest sense, gathering around him a community of disciples that was among the most luminous of the entire Egyptian desert. But his wisdom was hard-won: Poemen was not a saint who found the ascetic life easy. He suffered, he struggled, he wept, and out of that wrestling came counsel of the most practical and penetrating kind. 'Whatever you do, do it not with the boldness of one who knows, but with the humility of one who is learning.' The monthly Crucifixion and Abba Poemen belong together because both insist on the necessity of what the world calls failure as the precondition of what heaven calls glory.

St. Phanourios the Great Martyr is an interesting figure in the calendar: his relics were discovered in Rhodes in the fifteenth century, long after his martyrdom, and the rediscovery of a forgotten saint was treated by the Church as a new revelation of grace — as if God had preserved him in hiddenness for a specific moment and purpose. He has become the patron of those who seek lost things, and his intercessions are sought not only for lost objects but for lost people, lost paths, lost causes. On the twenty-seventh day of Nehase, with the monthly Crucifixion as the day's anchor, Phanourios speaks to the nature of redemption itself: Christ on the Cross is the God who came to find what was lost, to recover what had been hidden, to restore what had fallen. Every lost thing that is found by Phanourios's intercession is a small sacrament of the great Recovery.

The monthly Crucifixion, Abba Poemen, and Phanourios together make of this twenty-seventh day a day of depth and searching. The fast of Filseta has passed; the feasts of Tekle Haymanot and Joshua and the other great saints have been celebrated. Now the month draws toward its close, and the Cross stands again at the center: not as a rebuke but as a compass, orienting everything that has come before and everything that follows, reminding the Church that the glory it has celebrated is always a cruciform glory, always risen from the place of dying, always most true at the point where it is most costly.

28.title: 28 Nehase — St. Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob & The Assembly
28.narrative:
Nehase

The Holy Patriarchs Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob

St. Moses the Black (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Augustine of Hippo, Bishop and Confessor

The twenty-eighth day of Nehase gathers the three great Patriarchs of Israel into the Church's embrace: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the fathers of the faith, the ones to whom the promises were sworn, the tent-dwellers who walked with God in the full novelty of a relationship that had never before existed. The Ethiopian Church honors them not merely as historical ancestors of Judaism but as living members of the assembly of the righteous, present now in the fullness of the Promise whose shadow they inhabited on earth. The God who identified Himself to Moses as 'the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob' — and who is, as the Lord said, the God of the living and not the dead — is the same God whom the Ethiopian faithful worship in their liturgies. These three are not strangers but family, not Old Testament figures but members of the eternal household.

Abraham is the father of all who believe: he went out without knowing where he was going, which is the founding act of faith, the first and unrepeatable step into the unknown that all subsequent believers replay in their own way. Isaac was the child of impossibility who became the embodiment of the willingness to be offered — lying on the mountain, he was both the sacrifice and the one who was spared, a double icon of the Lamb of God. Jacob, the wrestler with the Angel, the one who limped away from Peniel with a blessing and a permanent wound, is the ancestor of all those who have been changed by their encounter with the divine. To have met God is to be permanently different; Jacob's limp is the Church's limp, the holy disability of those who have been in the Presence.

The monthly commemoration of St. Moses the Black, the Ethiopian robber-chief turned desert father and priest, is perfectly placed on this day of the Patriarchs. Moses was a man of Africa, of massive physical stature and volcanic temper, who underwent one of the most complete conversions in the Sayings of the Desert Fathers. He became a monk at Scetis, was ordained priest with great resistance from his own sense of unworthiness, and was martyred with his fellow monks by Berber raiders after refusing to flee. His wisdom is among the most quoted in the Apophthegmata: 'Stay in your cell, and your cell will teach you everything.' For the Ethiopian Church he is an ancestor in the faith in a peculiarly intimate sense — he is their kind, their color, their soil — and his holiness is a testimony that the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob is also the God of the Nile and the desert and the dark skin.

St. Augustine of Hippo brings North African Christianity to its highest theological pitch on this same day. Augustine — son of Monica, rhetorician of Carthage, bishop of Hippo, doctor of grace — was formed in the same African soil that formed Moses the Black, though in a different era and tradition. His Confessions are the most intimate record of a human soul's journey to God in the literature of the ancient Church; his City of God is the most comprehensive attempt to read the whole of human history through the lens of the Kingdom. On this twenty-eighth day of Nehase, Abraham the tent-dweller, Isaac the near-sacrifice, Jacob the wrestler, Moses the converted robber, and Augustine the converted rhetorician form an improbable and magnificent chorus of those who were found, changed, and made new by the God who seeks what is lost.

29.title: 29 Nehase — The Triple Feast & The Assembly
29.narrative:
Nehase

The Triple Feast: Maskal, Qal and Timqat (Monthly Commemoration)

St. John the Baptist (Beheading of)

The Forty Martyrs of Sebaste

The twenty-ninth day of Nehase carries the monthly Triple Feast, the simultaneous commemoration of Maskal (the Finding of the True Cross), Qal (the Covenant/Word), and Timqat (the Theophany/Baptism of the Lord) — three of the most exalted feasts of the Ethiopian liturgical year gathered into a single monthly observance that is unique to the Tewahedo tradition. This triple commemoration speaks to the Ethiopian Church's understanding that the central mysteries of salvation are not isolated events but a single luminous reality seen from three angles: the Cross by which we are redeemed, the Word by which we are addressed and summoned, and the Baptism by which we are incorporated into the death and resurrection of the Lord. Monthly these three are held up together as a triptych, a threefold icon of the one economy.

The Beheading of St. John the Baptist falls on this twenty-ninth day with a gravity that the Ethiopian calendar feels fully. John was the last and greatest of the prophets, the one who stood on the boundary between the Old Covenant and the New, the voice crying in the wilderness who had the singular honor of baptizing the Lord he proclaimed. His execution at the pleasure of a dancing girl's birthday wish is one of the most disturbing events in the Gospel narratives — a reminder that the Kingdom of God does not come in power and triumph but in apparent failure and ignominy, that the greatest of those born of women was beheaded in a dungeon for the sake of an adulterer's pride. The Church mourns John's beheading as a fast-day in many traditions, and the Ethiopian calendar holds it in deep solemnity.

The Forty Martyrs of Sebaste add a corporate dimension of incomparable power to this penultimate day of Nehase. Forty soldiers of the Roman legion stationed at Sebaste in Armenia, commanded under pain of death to sacrifice to the gods, instead stripped off their armor and walked out onto the frozen lake, refusing. Heaters were placed on the bank to tempt them to recant; one did, and ran to the bank, but the guard watching was so struck by what he saw — reportedly, a vision of crowns descending from heaven toward the remaining thirty-nine — that he stripped off his own clothes, declared himself a Christian, and took the fortieth place on the ice. They died of exposure, singing. The image of forty men singing on a frozen lake in the face of death is one of the Church's most perfect icons of the freedom that the Gospel gives.

On this twenty-ninth and penultimate day of Nehase, the Triple Feast, the Beheading of the Forerunner, and the Martyrs of Sebaste converge in a great act of liturgical recapitulation. The month has been a journey through the Cross (the monthly Crucifixion on the twenty-seventh), the Word (present throughout in the prophets and apostles), and the Baptism (present in every martyrdom, which is a baptism of blood). Tomorrow Nehase ends. But today, the Church stands at the summit of the month's final ascent and sees the whole of it spread out below: the light of Tabor, the long fast, the Assumption's glory, the great saints, the martyrs, the patriarchs — all of it gathered up and offered back to God in the one unending act of worship that the liturgical year enacts.

30.title: 30 Nehase — St. Malachi the Prophet & The Assembly
30.narrative:
Nehase

St. Malachi the Prophet

Sts. Alexander, John, and Paul, Patriarchs of Constantinople

St. Phineas (Phinehas) the Priest and Prophet

The thirtieth and final day of Nehase belongs to St. Malachi, the last of the prophets, the one whose name means 'my messenger' and whose book is the final word of the Hebrew prophetic canon — the last page before the long silence that preceded the Forerunner and the Lord. Malachi prophesied in the fifth century before Christ when the returnees from exile had rebuilt the Temple but had grown weary and complacent in their religion, bringing blemished offerings and going through the motions of worship without their hearts. His message cut: 'You have wearied the Lord with your words.' But his conclusion blazed with eschatological hope: 'Behold, I am sending my messenger, who will prepare the way before me' — words that the New Testament immediately applies to John the Baptist — and 'the sun of righteousness shall rise with healing in its wings.' On the last day of Nehase, which stands at the threshold of the intercalary days and the new year, Malachi's voice is exactly right: a word of sobriety about the quality of worship offered, and a word of blazing promise about what is coming.

The Patriarchs Alexander, John, and Paul of Constantinople are commemorated together on this final day — three bishops who presided over the New Rome in succession during the tumultuous fourth century, the era of Arianism and Nicene orthodoxy, of Councils and controversies. Alexander was the bishop who stood firm at Nicaea and who reportedly prayed that the Arian Arius would die before he could be restored to communion — and according to the tradition, his prayer was answered the night before the scheduled reconciliation. John his successor continued the Nicene witness. Paul was twice exiled by Arian emperors and finally strangled in exile. These three together represent the episcopate as it was meant to be: not a court appointment or an administrative post but a watchman's vocation, costly, contested, and defined by willingness to suffer for the faith once delivered.

St. Phineas — Phinehas — the grandson of Aaron and great high priest whose name appears in the book of Numbers and the Psalms, stands as the final Old Testament voice of Nehase, completing the great arc that began with Adam on the first day. Phinehas was zealous for the holiness of God at a moment of national apostasy, and his zeal — however difficult it is to read across the distance of the centuries — was credited to him as righteousness, and the covenant of the priesthood was renewed through his line. On the last day of the month, Phinehas stands as a question to the community that has kept Nehase: Has the journey of this month produced a deeper zeal, a cleaner offering, a more attentive heart?

Nehase ends tonight. The month of the Transfiguration and the Assumption, the month of the great Filseta fast, the month of Tekle Haymanot's departure and the monthly Cross and the Patriarchs of the Faith — all of it closes on the lips of the last prophet and the echo of his question. But his final word was not judgment; it was promise: the sun of righteousness, rising. The new year is at hand. The intercalary days follow, the Pagumen that is the thirteenth small month, and then the New Year, Enkutatash, the feast of finding. Malachi knew what comes after silence: the messenger, and then the Lord himself, suddenly coming to His temple. The Church that has kept the whole of Nehase in watchfulness is ready to hear the sound of His feet.
